Everyone froze. The seam under her hand pulsed once, hard.\\n\\nMel’s pen stopped. “That wasn’t feed. That was expansion.”\\n\\n“Backflow,” Tiffani said. “Pressure building from the Crown line.”\\n\\nJhace threw his kit open, grabbed the release valve wrench. “We vent it.”\\n\\n“You open that without brace, you’ll shear the plate!” Mel barked.\\n\\n“Then we brace it!” He drove another wedge, deeper, metal ringing like struck bells.\\n\\nTiffani grabbed the Lume, cut light to zero, then reopened to full to check feedback. “Still rising!”\\n\\nJhace jammed the wrench on the valve. “Three turns!”\\n\\nMel shouted over the hiss, “Half-turn only!”\\n\\n“Three!”\\n\\nHe spun it once, twice. The seam screamed, then sighed—long, hollow. Steam shot six feet into the air, burning everything in reach.\\n\\nTiffani flinched back, gloved hands up. Jhace’s left sleeve caught moisture that scalded through the fabric.\\n\\nThen the sound shifted: the deep, low exhale of weight finding balance.\\n\\nMel crouched again, pressed her palm to the plate. “Pressure’s gone. It’s breathing normal.”\\n\\nJhace sat back, shaking his burned arm. “We’re clear?”\\n\\nShe nodded. “HALT lifts at Bell Nine.”\\n\\nBrighlee’s voice cracked across the rise: “Witnessed!”\\n\\nTiffani let out a sound halfway between a laugh and a gasp. “You’re an idiot.”\\n\\n“Functional idiot,” he said.\\n\\n“Still counts.”\\n\\nShe took his wrist, rolled the sleeve up. Skin blistered pink. “Clinic, now.”\\n\\nHe nodded once, too tired to argue.\\n\\nMel stood, wrote the clearance in her ledger with hands still shaking. “I’m signing this one myself.”\\n\\nShe posted the slip under the blue banner. The paper soaked through but held. “Policy note: overbind feed must split before marriage. Write that down, Brighlee.”\\n\\nBrighlee scribbled. “Noted.”\\n\\nCarts began to move again, slow, careful. The crowd dispersed with the reverent silence only near-misses earn.\\n\\nJhace stayed seated, staring at the line. “We nearly lost it.”\\n\\nTiffani sat beside him, shoulder to shoulder. “We didn’t.”\\n\\nHer hand brushed his burned one, deliberate, firm. His breathing evened. That was enough.\\n\\n\\nContinuity:\\nArtifact — **NARROWVALE RISE HALT — Bells 5–9** Full structural shutdown under emergency order. Dual-vein overbind corrected via manual trace and vent. Witnessed clearance granted by Conclave Setter Mel Quarr. Policy update: overbind feeds require split pre-marriage; HALT protocols enforce local brace confirmation before vent. Costs: burn (left forearm, superficial), one wrench warped.\\nImage — Blue banners snapping wet in the cooling air while steam curled around a single blackened glove abandoned by the valve, the bridge carrying a slow breath beneath it.\\n\\n---\\n\\n# Chapter 9: Slow Bell at Mirecourt\\n\\nBell Ten dragged like a drunk cart. Nothing wanted to move. The fog was the thick kind that collected sweat from anyone dumb enough to work in it. Jhace hadn’t slept since Narrowvale. His burned arm still stung, wrapped tight under his sleeve.\\n\\nThe summons had come anyway. *Minor leak, Mirecourt span.* Nothing in the city was ever minor.\\n\\nHe and Tiffani reached the gate face to find condensation dripping from the inner rail, slow and steady. Not a break, not yet. The sound of it was worse—a hollow *tick, tick* between the Bells like a wound that couldn’t decide if it was open.\\n\\nTiffani crouched. Her face was gray from sleeplessness. “Overbind residue. Narrowvale runoff hit the lower feeds. Pressure’s wrong.”\\n\\nHe set his tool pack down, slower than usual. His hand didn’t want to close around the latch. “We shouldn’t be working.”\\n\\nShe didn’t look up. “You say that every time you’re bleeding.”\\n\\n“Not every time.”\\n\\n“Name one you didn’t.”\\n\\nHe didn’t answer.\\n\\nBrighlee trudged up the incline, eyes ringed dark. “Work order four-eight-one. Leak containment, Mirecourt. Bell Ten to Bell Eleven.” Her voice rasped. “No witness posted yet.”\\n\\n“Fine,” Tiffani said. “We’ll make one when it matters.”\\n\\nJhace pulled his glove tighter, swallowed the dry taste in his mouth. “You handle trace. I’ll brace.”\\n\\n“Arm still burned?”\\n\\n“It moves.”\\n\\n“Not the same as healed.”\\n\\n“Closer than dead.”\\n\\nShe sighed, then crouched beside the line. “One hand on home.”\\n\\nHe found the anchor post. “One hand on home.”\\n\\nThe first wedge slipped from his fingers. Too slick. He wiped his palm on his coat, tried again. The second bit.\\n\\n“Shallow leak,” Tiffani said, eyes on the Lume. “Midline pulse, four counts low.”\\n\\nHe could feel it through the plate—a heartbeat out of rhythm.\\n\\n“Half-beat Slake,” she said.\\n\\nHe nodded. His movements were slower now, the kind that come when muscles haven’t slept. The copper line glowed faintly wet; his reflection in it looked like someone older.\\n\\nShe whispered counts as he drew. “One. Two. Half.”\\n\\nThe wet hissed. The seam brightened, steadied. The tick stopped.\\n\\n“Got it,” he said.\\n\\nShe didn’t answer.\\n\\nHe looked up. Her face had gone blank. The Lume slipped from her hand, rolled once, hit the stone.\\n\\n“Tiff.”\\n\\nNothing. Her eyes blinked once, slow. “Shade wrong,” she murmured. “Four instead of two.”\\n\\n“Too hot.”\\n\\nShe nodded, dazed. “I fixed it.”\\n\\nHe reached out, steadying her wrist. Her pulse jumped under his thumb.\\n\\n“Stay,” he said.\\n\\nShe inhaled, slow, and color returned to her cheeks. “I’m fine.”\\n\\n“Liar.”\\n\\n“Better liar than corpse.”\\n\\nBrighlee stood back, watching like she wasn’t sure if she should record or intervene. “Do you want me to—”\\n\\n“Keep writing,” Tiffani said.\\n\\nThey moved in silence for a few minutes, fixing what the last sleepless shift had left crooked. The line dried evenly.\\n\\n“You sure the leak’s sealed?” Jhace asked.\\n\\nTiffani lifted the Lume, checked the feedback. “Trace reads steady. If it fails, it’ll do it politely.”\\n\\n“Polite’s new.”\\n\\n“Maybe the city’s learning.”\\n\\nHe sat back against the post, wiping sweat with his good sleeve. “You’re shaking.”\\n\\n“So are you.”\\n\\n“Then we match.”\\n\\nShe smiled, faint but there. “Don’t make poetry out of exhaustion.”\\n\\n“Just naming what’s real.”\\n\\nMel Quarr appeared at the top of the ramp, looking as tired as the rest. “Report.”\\n\\n“Minor leak, sealed,” Tiffani said. “No hold. Feed equalized.”\\n\\nMel knelt, pressed her hand to the plate. “It’s holding.” She looked at Jhace’s arm. “You working injured again?”\\n\\n“Policy says clinic after shift.”\\n\\n“Policy also says don’t die for copper.”\\n\\nHe shrugged. “I’m following half.”\\n\\nMel stood, scrawled her signature. “Witnessed.” She hesitated before clipping the slip. “You two need rest more than the city needs another repair.”\\n\\n“The city disagrees,” Tiffani said.\\n\\nMel gave her a look that was almost pity. “The city always disagrees. That’s why we have clinics.”\\n\\nBrighlee pinned the form to the board, ink smudged from damp air. “Work order four-eight-one complete. Bell Eleven.”\\n\\n“Costs?” Mel asked.\\n\\n“Burn reopening,” Jhace said.\\n\\n“Dehydration,” Tiffani added.\\n\\nMel wrote them both, slow. “Clinic. I’ll send a slip.”\\n\\nThey didn’t argue this time.\\n\\nWhen the others left, Jhace stayed a minute longer. The bridge carried a steady pulse under fog.\\n\\nTiffani leaned against the rail beside him. “You’re bleeding through the wrap.”\\n\\nHe glanced down. A small patch of red, barely showing. “That’s proof I’m alive.”\\n\\nShe leaned her head against the same rail, eyes half-closed. “Alive’s overrated.”\\n\\nHe smiled. “Then we’ll be mediocre together.”\\n\\nShe didn’t laugh, but her shoulder brushed his. His tremor eased. That counted.\\n\\nThey left the Lume burning low on the post, light catching the still wet line like a thin scar that refused to fade.\\n\\n\\nContinuity:\\nArtifact — **MIRECOURT SPAN — Bell 10–11** Minor leak containment. Feed imbalance from Narrowvale runoff corrected via manual trace. No local hold required. Witness: Mel Quarr. Costs: reopened burn, dehydration, Shade mis-set corrected. Policy note: consecutive shifts exceeding four Bells require mandatory clinic rest.\\nImage — The span under fog carrying a slow, tired rhythm while a thin red patch darkens the wrap around a setter’s hand and the low Lume flickers against steel gone cold.\\n\\n---\\n\\n# Chapter 10: Mobel at the Table (Interlude)\\n\\nBell Two chimed hollow through the Mercers’ Quarter, each note sliding through the lattice rafters above the weighing floor. Mobel had been there since dawn, coat off, sleeves rolled, the kind of tired that doesn’t show until it stops moving.\\n\\nThe scales in front of him were old Crown brass, polished to dull gold. They didn’t lie often, but they could be coaxed. The night auditors had caught three weight sets marked light in the middle coin. Not enough to hang a merchant, enough to make the Conclave twitch.\\n\\nHe placed one suspect weight on the pan, thumbed the notch along its rim. “Filed too deep,” he said to his clerk. “Half-grain shave.”\\n\\nThe clerk squinted. “Still legal?”\\n\\nMobel didn’t answer. He flicked the weight off the pan. It hit the floor with a hollow ring. “Not when it starts breeding.”\\n\\nThe smell of old oil filled the air as apprentices cleaned their scales. Every trade table in the room carried its own seal—the droplet for Couriers, the hammer for Forgewrights, the two crossed wedges newly added for Locks and Loads. That mark had cost Jhace a burn and half the city a rulebook rewrite.\\n\\nHe checked the next weight. The brass surface bore faint scrape lines, deliberate. He turned it over and read the faint initials carved into the base. “Harnet.”\\n\\nThe clerk’s eyes widened. “He’s senior.”\\n\\n“Was,” Mobel said. “Post the charge.”\\n\\nThe clerk hesitated. “Sir, it’s only half a grain—”\\n\\nMobel’s voice stayed even. “Half a grain turns into famine when multiplied. Post it.”\\n\\nThe clerk went pale but moved, writing the notice on a strip of wax paper. Mobel pulled the ink stamp from his belt, Tempermarked the sheet, and clipped it to the board above the scales. The room stilled. Every merchant in sight turned toward the sound.\\n\\n“Official?” one called.\\n\\n“Filed under Bell Two,” Mobel said.\\n\\nSomeone muttered, “Conclave doesn’t usually bother with half-grains.”\\n\\n“They do now.”\\n\\nHe reached for the next weight, turned it, sniffed the faint tang of fresh polish. Too clean. He scraped a knife edge across it. Bright line. “Fresh file,” he said.\\n\\nA Forgewright approached, face dark with anger. “We recalibrate weekly. No harm in keeping tools smooth.”\\n\\nMobel held up the knife. “Smooth isn’t the same as light.” He balanced the weight against a verified measure. The pan dipped slow, traitorous. “See?”\\n\\nThe forgewright looked away. “Barely a whisper.”\\n\\nMobel nodded once, calm. “That whisper just cost you your table.”\\n\\nHe wrote the notice himself, no clerk this time: *Forgery of official weights, Harnet Forge Cooperative. License revoked pending audit. Seals to be melted public.*\\n\\nHe signed it, pressed the Tempermark, and handed it to the clerk. “Fire the furnace.”\\n\\nThe apprentices dragged a small crucible from the side wall, set it on the coals, pumped the bellows until the air shimmered. The smell of heating metal cut through the damp.\\n\\nMobel took the forged weights one by one, dropped them into the pot. They sizzled as moisture burned off, then sagged into liquid. The room filled with quiet except for the hiss of betrayal cooking.\\n\\nThe Forgewright spoke again, voice shaking. “You’re making a show of this.”\\n\\nMobel didn’t look up. “No. A record.”\\n\\n“Same thing.”\\n\\nHe stirred the molten brass with a rod, watching the color shift from yellow to red to dull silver. “Show fades. Record stays.”\\n\\nWhen the last of the fake weights had gone under, he tilted the crucible slightly, poured the liquid into a flat mold shaped like a wedge. “New symbol,” he said. “Half-grain truth.”\\n\\nThe clerk stared. “What do we do with it?”\\n\\n“Post it on the Table.”\\n\\nThey nailed the cooled wedge to the central board between the trade seals, the scarred metal still faintly warm. It read: *Mass must match mark. Deviance voids claim.*\\n\\nMobel looked around the room. No one met his eyes. “Back to work,” he said.\\n\\nThe merchants dispersed. The clatter of weights returned, but softer, careful now.\\n\\nWhen the last Bell of morning rang, the clerk handed him the day’s ledger. “Three confessions filed already,” she said. “Two from vendors who weren’t even questioned.”\\n\\nMobel nodded, too tired for satisfaction. “Guilt travels faster than ink.”\\n\\nHe sat for a while after the room cleared, listening to the slow tick of cooling metal. He wasn’t proud. Just done. He wiped his hands on his coat, left the burned smell for the next shift to inherit.\\n\\nOutside, a new placard hung on the Mercers’ archway—one he hadn’t ordered but approved by existing rule. It carried Corlexi’s twin-wedge symbol above the words *DOUBLE CHECK. DOUBLE PAY.* He smiled once, small, then vanished into the street crowd before anyone could think it was approval.\\n\\n\\nContinuity:\\nArtifact — **MERCERS’ TABLE AUDIT — Bell 2–4** Forgery of trade weights confirmed. Harnet Forge Cooperative stripped of license. Counterfeit measures melted into single “Half-Grain Wedge,” displayed publicly at Mercers’ Table. Policy expansion: daily double verification of all Conclave-approved measures.\\nImage — A single wedge of cooled brass nailed to the trade board, edges uneven, letters burned in by hand—heat ghosting through the metal while honest weights tick in cautious rhythm around it.\\n\\n---\\n\\n# Chapter 11: Vein Drift at Old Barrick\\n\\nBell Six began with rain that refused to quit. Every gutter in Old Barrick bled the same brown water, the kind that carried grit sharp enough to chew soles. Jhace trudged through it anyway, coat soaked, mind half-asleep. The summons had read simple: *Feed drift reported beneath Barrick arch. Confirm Vein integrity.* Simple meant dangerous.\\n\\nTiffani waited under the arch with her case open and her patience closed. “Feed’s running wrong,” she said as soon as he got close. “Map shows three lines. I’m reading four.”\\n\\nHe crouched beside her, wiped water from his eyes. The metal under them vibrated soft but uneven. “Someone’s been splicing.”\\n\\n“Unauthorized?”\\n\\n“Unauthorized, stupid, or both.”\\n\\nBrighlee set her ledger box on a dry patch—there weren’t many—and unfolded the Conclave diagram. “Official routing stops at Bell Yard,” she said. “This fourth line isn’t on record.”\\n\\nTiffani’s Lume flicked against the steel face. “There. New weld, bad angle. You can still smell the resin.”\\n\\nJhace touched the seam; it flexed under pressure. “Not Veinwright work.”\\n\\n“Then whose?”\\n\\nHe looked up toward the row of cheap workshops hanging off the arch face. “Barrick guild leases to anyone with a torch and a prayer.”\\n\\n“Then we pray faster,” she said.\\n\\nThe vibration deepened. The ground gave a little under their knees.\\n\\n“Shut down feed before it chews through the arch.”\\n\\nBrighlee fumbled the comm key. “Conclave control, Barrick line—Vein drift, unregistered splice, request partial bleed—”\\n\\nThe line popped once, dead.\\n\\n“Signal’s out,” she said.\\n\\nJhace stood, scanning the arch. “Manual cut then. Valve’s three meters under street. Get me the pry.”\\n\\nTiffani handed it over, already lowering the Lume toward the street joint. Water rushed around their boots, gray with oil.\\n\\nHe wedged the bar between plates, pulled. The valve cap lifted half a finger. “Blocked.”\\n\\n“Rust?”\\n\\n“No, resin. Whoever spliced this sealed the control with the same filth.”\\n\\nTiffani exhaled, long and angry. “They overbound to siphon flow. You can see the heat scar from a fake Tempermark.”\\n\\nHe followed her gaze. The symbol was there, crooked, shallow.\\n\\n“Mercers again?”\\n\\n“Looks cheaper than that.”\\n\\nThe vibration turned into a shudder, steady and wrong.\\n\\n“Brace it,” she said.\\n\\n“I can’t—valve’s stuck.”\\n\\nShe grabbed her Veil case, ripped out a Shade skin, slapped it over the joint. “It won’t hold long.”\\n\\n“Long’s relative.” He pried again. The bar slipped. His burned hand screamed. He bit back sound and drove it again.\\n\\nThe valve gave, half-turn. A rush of hot air vented out, carrying the stink of burnt polymer.\\n\\n“Feed drop?” he asked.\\n\\nTiffani watched the Lume gauge. “Down ten. Still high.”\\n\\n“Second turn.”\\n\\nHe forced the bar, pain flaring through his arm. “That’s it. Thirty percent bleed.”\\n\\nThe vibration began to level. The Veil over the joint quivered but held.\\n\\nShe crouched beside it. “Someone built this line to feed an unlisted forge.”\\n\\nHe wiped water from his face. “Where?”\\n\\n“Follow the warmth.” She pressed her palm to the stone. Heat traced north. “Two spans up. Under that shop with the green door.”\\n\\n“Of course it’s painted green,” he muttered.\\n\\nThey slogged through ankle water to the door. The forge inside was dark but hot. Tools scattered. The feed pipe split into a small coupling feeding a single crucible. The floor was scorched.\\n\\n“Illegal tap,” Tiffani said.\\n\\n“Improper angle, wrong alloys, counterfeit mark,” Jhace said. “It’s a miracle they didn’t melt half the street.”\\n\\n“Still might.”\\n\\nHe reached for his trace stick, pulled a wet line across the pipe joint. Steam hissed. The fake weld separated cleanly, exposing the stolen vein.\\n\\n“Cut?” she asked.\\n\\n“Cut.” He drove a plug into the open end and Slaked it. The pipe groaned and cooled.\\n\\nBrighlee ducked in, soaked through. “Control restored. They felt the bleed upstream.”\\n\\n“Good.” Jhace pointed at the crucible. “Mark it for confiscation.”\\n\\nMel Quarr entered from the alley door, expression unreadable. “You two have a talent for finding trouble.”\\n\\n“Trouble builds itself,” Tiffani said. “We just trip over it first.”\\n\\nMel knelt by the severed pipe, ran a finger along the cut. “Clean. You trace that in a flood?”\\n\\nJhace nodded. “Twice.”\\n\\n“You’ll get your name in a policy again if you keep fixing other people’s greed.”\\n\\nHe didn’t answer.\\n\\nMel stood, signed Brighlee’s page. “Forge closed, feed sealed. Witnessed.”\\n\\nBrighlee clipped the paper to the doorpost, ink bleeding in the wet.\\n\\nMel looked up at the glowing arch above them, light bending off rain. “You know, this city’s not tired because it breaks. It’s tired because it has to hide the breaks before anyone sees.”\\n\\nTiffani said quietly, “Then we keep hiding them.”\\n\\nMel gave a thin smile. “That’s why you’re still alive.”\\n\\nJhace leaned against the wall, exhaustion visible even through grit. “For now.”\\n\\nTiffani caught his sleeve. “Stay on your feet. Clinic next.”\\n\\nHe nodded. “Policy.”\\n\\nThe rain thickened again, washing soot from the door. The counterfeit Tempermark softened and slid down the stone, leaving a streak that looked almost like a tear.\\n\\n\\nContinuity:\\nArtifact — **OLD BARRICK VEIN DRIFT — Bell 6–8** Unauthorized splice discovered beneath Barrick arch. Counterfeit Tempermark identified and excised. Feed bleed and rebalancing performed under flood conditions. Witness: Mel Quarr. Costs: burn strain, fatigue, bar bent. Policy update: random valve inspections extended to Lower and Middle Barrick guild leases; unregistered feeds subject to confiscation.\\nImage — Rain running down the arch face in streaks that dissolve a fake Tempermark until nothing’s left but wet stone, heat faint underneath like a secret too tired to hide.\\n\\n---\\n\\n# Chapter 12: Crossline at Ledger Row\\n\\nBell Three came in dry for once, cold air cutting through the Underface like clean glass. It was almost pleasant until Jhace saw the smoke curling from under the boards along Ledger Row. The smell wasn’t fire—it was burnt resin and oil, the kind of heat you only got when Veins fought each other.\\n\\nTiffani was already kneeling by the conduit hatch, hair pulled back, eyes narrow from fatigue and focus. “Feed maps say one line,” she said, voice tight. “There are two.”\\n\\nJhace dropped to a crouch beside her, pressing his good hand against the iron face. The vibration was wild. Two distinct pulses, crossing in opposite rhythms, one slow and hot, one quick and cold. “Crossline. Someone overlaid without bleed.”\\n\\n“That’s not possible without Conclave clearance.”\\n\\n“Or a bribe.”\\n\\nBrighlee arrived half-running, panting. “Work order five-zero-seven, feed interference, Ledger Row. No hold posted.” She scanned the crowd gathering at the edge. “We might need one.”\\n\\n“Not yet,” Tiffani said. “Half the quarter’s power runs through here. If we stop it, clinics freeze before Bell Six.”\\n\\nJhace nodded once. “We keep it live. Slow fix.”\\n\\n“Slow’ll kill us if the Veins argue harder.”\\n\\n“They’re already arguing.”\\n\\nHe pried the hatch open. The inside glowed faintly red-blue, two colors flickering at war. The Veins had touched somewhere beneath, fusing in a way that was both alive and dying.\\n\\n“Shade three,” he said.\\n\\nTiffani adjusted the Lume, washing the chamber in muted amber light. “There’s a junction below the ledger feed, looks welded by hand.”\\n\\nHe leaned in, saw the crude cross of metal binding both veins together. “That’s not a weld. That’s an apology.”\\n\\n“Someone knew it was wrong and hoped it’d hold.”\\n\\n“Hope isn’t a method.”\\n\\nHe reached in with the trace rod, testing. Sparks spat up the length of it.\\n\\n“Don’t touch the join,” she said sharply. “You’ll wake it.”\\n\\n“Too late.” The vibration shifted. Both pulses synchronized for one heartbeat, then slipped again, a deep, grinding pressure that made the floor shake.\\n\\nBrighlee stumbled back, ledger clutched to her chest. “That sounded like—”\\n\\n“Backflow,” Jhace said. “It’s feeding both directions.”\\n\\n“Then it’s gonna eat itself.”\\n\\n“Not if we teach it manners.”\\n\\nHe pulled his gloves tight and slid the trace rod deeper. Heat bit through the leather. “Give me wet, low pressure.”\\n\\nTiffani passed him the bladder. “Half-beat Slake.”\\n\\n“Half-beat,” he echoed.\\n\\nHe eased a thin line along the hot vein first. Steam poured out, hissed, then bent. The color dimmed. “It’s listening.”\\n\\n“Cold side next,” she said.\\n\\nHe shifted the rod, drew a second line that ran parallel but in reverse direction. The two wet trails hissed where they met, forming a pale seam.\\n\\n“It’s working,” Brighlee said softly.\\n\\n“Not yet.” Jhace pressed his burned palm flat against the plate. The pain kept him sharp. “Give me Shade one. I want the edges honest.”\\n\\nTiffani dropped the light, fine and close. The flicker slowed. The noise eased into a low, even pulse.\\n\\n“That’s the sweet spot,” she whispered.\\n\\nHe didn’t move. “It’s temporary. The weld underneath is still there.”\\n\\nShe nodded. “Then we peel it.”\\n\\nThey worked in silence, two sets of hands moving like one, prying at the crooked cross until the metal gave with a faint groan. Beneath it, the two Veins lay clean but raw. Jhace laid a single wet bead between them, sealing the scar.\\n\\nMel Quarr appeared in the doorway, coat dripping from street wash. “What did you two touch this time?”\\n\\n“Crossline under Ledger,” Tiffani said. “Unauthorized weld.”\\n\\nMel crouched, studying the seal. “You joined them?”\\n\\n“Separated them,” Jhace said. “Then taught them to agree.”\\n\\nMel’s mouth tightened. “That’s not procedure.”\\n\\n“It’s what worked.”\\n\\nShe pressed her ear to the conduit wall, listening. “Sounds stable.” She straightened, slower than usual. “You realize if that weld had held another Bell, it would’ve burned through to the counting halls?”\\n\\n“Then we’re early,” Jhace said.\\n\\nMel smiled without humor. “Luck counts as skill in my book. Witnessed.”\\n\\nBrighlee wrote fast, her handwriting going sloppy from wet fingers. “Ledger Row, crossline repair, dual trace confirmed.”\\n\\n“Add note,” Mel said. “Unauthorized weld under review. Materials confiscated.”\\n\\n“Already did.”\\n\\nThe rain outside picked up, washing soot down the gutters.\\n\\nTiffani leaned back, rubbing her face. “That makes four unregistered modifications in a week.”\\n\\nJhace wiped his tools clean. “Pattern’s too neat to be chance.”\\n\\n“You think it’s one guild?”\\n\\n“I think it’s everyone who thinks they can hide a shortcut.”\\n\\nMel stepped toward the door. “Write it down,” she said. “Patterns matter.”\\n\\nBrighlee nodded. “Filed.”\\n\\nThe crowd began to move again, carts edging forward as the tension lifted. A vendor shouted something about late deliveries. The world resumed its noise.\\n\\nJhace packed his tools. “You realize we’re just bailing a sinking ship with prettier buckets.”\\n\\nTiffani closed her case. “Then we make the buckets shine.”\\n\\nHe looked at her, the corner of his mouth twitching. “You’d polish a corpse.”\\n\\n“Only if it earned it.”\\n\\nMel lingered in the doorway, watching them. “You two keep that attitude and the Conclave will write you into the walls.”\\n\\n“Then at least they’ll stay up,” Jhace said.\\n\\nMel left first. The others followed.\\n\\nWhen the street cleared, the repaired conduit steamed softly in the cold air. Two lines, once at war, now carried a common rhythm under the stone.\\n\\n\\nContinuity:\\nArtifact — **LEDGER ROW CROSSLINE — Bell 3–5** Unauthorized dual-feed junction discovered beneath accounting district. Illegal weld excised, dual trace and reseal executed under live flow. Feed stabilized. Witness: Mel Quarr. Costs: fatigue, minor reinjury (hand), tool heat wear. Policy: cross-feed junctions require dual oversight and random audit within five Bells of modification notice.\\nImage — Steam rising from twin Veins under the street, the wet seam between them glowing faint blue in the dark, carrying a held breath between agreement and surrender.\\n\\n---\\n\\n# Chapter 13: The Day Everything Moved\\n\\nBell One started with silence. Then the city breathed wrong.\\n\\nFrom the Underface to the Crown, pipes groaned, floors flexed, and every Tempermark board across Te’Oga flickered blue-black, the color no one wanted to see. **SYSTEM STRAIN—VEIN UNSTABLE.**\\n\\nJhace felt it before he heard it—a deep tremor under his boots while crossing Trine Arch. The bridge had survived three collapses and one rebuild. It wasn’t built for this.\\n\\nTiffani’s voice cut through the noise on his comm. “Four districts reporting pulse conflict. Feed rates rising across every bound junction.”\\n\\n“That’s everything we touched in the last twenty rounds,” he said.\\n\\n“Not just ours. Corlexi’s network flagged too. Barrick, Mirecourt, even Narrowvale.”\\n\\n“Meaning?”\\n\\n“The city’s veins are syncing.”\\n\\nHe froze. “That’s not possible without a chain relay.”\\n\\n“Or without all of them hitting their stress limit at once.”\\n\\nThe Bells shifted to emergency pattern—two fast, one long. HALT banners unfurled on every visible post. The entire city stopped.\\n\\nBrighlee’s voice crackled through the static. “Conclave calling all active Setters. Full coordination. Each pair covers previous sites. Jhace, Tiffani—you take Narrowvale and Blackgate. Priority one.”\\n\\nHe ran.\\n\\nThe streets had emptied except for the workers standing under blue light. The smell of burnt flux drifted from the under pipes. At Narrowvale Rise, steam poured from the vent caps where they’d joined the twin Veins. The line glowed faint orange—alive but choking.\\n\\nTiffani arrived a beat later, soaked through. “We knew it would come back,” she said.\\n\\n“Not like this.”\\n\\nShe crouched at the valve, Lume set to low. “Pressure doubled. Feed trying to push both ways again.”\\n\\nHe opened his kit. “We vent one side.”\\n\\n“Pick wrong and we flood half the Crown.”\\n\\n“Better than losing the whole plate.”\\n\\n“Better for who?”\\n\\nHe didn’t answer. The seam under his palm thrummed hard enough to rattle teeth. “Left side’s rising faster.”\\n\\n“Then right side vents.”\\n\\n“Agreed.”\\n\\nThey moved together, no words after that. Tiffani braced the wrench while he set the wedge. The metal screamed but obeyed. Steam burst upward, hot enough to lift her braid.\\n\\n“Drop two!” he shouted.\\n\\nShe twisted. The valve half-turned, jammed, turned again. The pressure line bent but didn’t break.\\n\\n“Pulse steady?”\\n\\nShe checked. “Mostly.”\\n\\n“Mostly keeps us alive.”\\n\\nBehind them, Mel Quarr arrived, her coat flaring with the wind. “Every site’s flaring,” she said. “Conclave can’t keep up.”\\n\\n“Then we hold our ground,” Jhace said.\\n\\nMel crouched, palm on the line. “Pulse frequency’s wrong. It’s synchronizing.”\\n\\n“Synthesizing?”\\n\\n“The Veins are talking to each other. Whatever you did here at Narrowvale started a pattern.”\\n\\nTiffani looked up. “Then the fix propagated.”\\n\\n“Or infected,” Mel said.\\n\\nAnother pulse hit, stronger. The line under their hands glowed white-hot.\\n\\n“Shade five,” Jhace said.\\n\\nTiffani dialed it down. “We can’t trace under this much heat.”\\n\\n“Then we bleed it slow. Better to lose heat than life.”\\n\\nBrighlee’s voice broke through the comm again. “Jhace—Blackgate just failed. The wet trace you set six rounds back cracked wide. You’re nearest.”\\n\\nHe looked at Tiffani. “We go.”\\n\\n“You can’t split,” Mel said. “You’ll lose both.”\\n\\n“Not if we divide.” He handed Tiffani his second bladder. “You hold this line. You know it as well as I do.”\\n\\nShe caught it, eyes hard. “You come back.”\\n\\n“If I don’t, post clean.”\\n\\n“Don’t get poetic.”\\n\\n“Not planning to.”\\n\\nHe took off toward Blackgate, every step pounding with the pulse beneath.\\n\\nThe gate face glowed when he reached it. The wet trace seam from the first job shimmered like a wound reopened. Carts stood abandoned, their wheels sunk in the softening road. The droplet Tempermark flickered in and out of existence.\\n\\nJhace dropped to one knee, breath hard. “Old friend,” he muttered. “You held longer than you should have.”\\n\\nHe cut the old Veil with his knife, peeled it back. The wet line underneath still traced true but cracked in two places. He set his last wedge at the shoulder, pressed until it caught, then drew new wet across the gap. The copper hissed and flared white.\\n\\n“Hold,” he whispered.\\n\\nThe citywide pulse beat through the ground—the whole place breathing in sync, every Vein thudding in time like a single heart.\\n\\nTiffani’s voice on comm: “Narrowvale stable. Feed bleed successful.”\\n\\n“Blackgate stabilizing,” he said. “Barely.”\\n\\nMel’s voice: “Barrick feed’s dead. Corlexi’s holding containment there. The Bore’s ring is flashing red.”\\n\\nJhace frowned. “The Bore hasn’t flashed since—”\\n\\n“Since it decided not to end the world,” she said.\\n\\n“Then it’s paying attention again.”\\n\\nThe pressure built until sound turned physical. Every signboard shook, ink flaking off like dust.\\n\\nHe pressed both hands to the line, eyes closed. “One hand on home,” he whispered, but used two anyway.\\n\\nThe pulse stuttered. Then it steadied.\\n\\nSteam vented through the whole undercity, white sheets rising. The Bells fell silent one by one until only the deep Conclave bell kept beating.\\n\\nWhen the noise finally faded, the wet line under his hands stayed dark and cool. The light along the droplet icon steadied blue.\\n\\n“Report,” Tiffani said.\\n\\nHe took a breath. “Blackgate held.”\\n\\nMel came back over the comm. “City-wide pulse synchronized and dropped. All feeds cooling. HALT clears at Bell Ten.”\\n\\nBrighlee added, voice cracking: “Conclave will record this as the Great Sync. No losses reported.”\\n\\nJhace laughed once, soft. “First time that’s ever true.”\\n\\nHe sat down on the wet stone. His glove stuck to the plate; when he peeled it off, skin came with it.\\n\\nTiffani found him before the Bell finished its last note. “You look terrible.”\\n\\n“You sound surprised.”\\n\\nShe crouched, laid a hand on his shoulder. “Stay.”\\n\\nHe did. The tremor under his skin eased.\\n\\nAbove them, Corlexi’s new double-wedge mark gleamed from the nearest board, dry in the rising sunlight.\\n\\n\\nContinuity:\\nArtifact — **THE GREAT SYNC — Bell 1–10** Simultaneous Vein synchronization spanning Te’Oga. Cross-feed, overbind, and prior manual traces entered resonance, stabilizing into unified pulse cycle. No structural losses. Emergency HALT rescinded at Bell Ten. Witnesses: Jhace brast, Tiffani veld, Mel Quarr, Corlexi. Costs: citywide fatigue, burns (Jhace), Narrowvale vein strain. Policy: synchronization thresholds logged; dynamic pulse monitoring before each Bell.\\nImage — Steam lifting from every street vent at once, sunlight cutting through it until the whole city shines like tempered metal—scarred, cooling, and still whole.\\n\\n---\\n\\n# Chapter 14: Clinic Rest Policy (Interlude)\\n\\nBell Five opened soft, rain whispering against clinic glass. The city was quiet—too quiet for Te’Oga. The Great Sync had drained everyone. Even the pipes breathed slower.\\n\\nSalee sat at her desk by the eastern window, a single ledger open, stylus steady. Her job was dull, which was why it mattered. Someone had to convert panic into law. The Conclave’s new order lay in front of her: *Mandatory rest interval for all registered Setters after major harmonization events. Minimum duration: one Bell. Paid.*\\n\\nShe had written worse.\\n\\nOutside, workers filed past the courtyard on slow feet. Their gait told her the truth before the numbers did. Nobody had stopped since the city nearly folded itself two Bells ago. Jhace’s name was on three of the emergency slips pinned to her wall. Tiffani’s on two. None cleared for clinic rest.\\n\\nSalee pushed the papers aside and stood, back popping. The clinic smelled of wet linen and alcohol. Every bed full, most with people asleep in work coats, tools still belted.\\n\\nShe found the medic in charge, a sharp little man named Isten who looked like he’d fought the rain and lost. “How many still running on post-sync adrenaline?”\\n\\n“All of them.”\\n\\n“Vitals?”\\n\\n“Stable enough to fool themselves.”\\n\\n“Feed them water. Mark the worst for sedation if they try to walk out.”\\n\\nHe raised an eyebrow. “On whose order?”\\n\\n“Mine.” She handed him the stamped form. “Policy’s live.”\\n\\nHe read it, eyes widening. “Paid rest?”\\n\\n“Conclave approved at Bell Three. You post it or I will.”\\n\\nHe grinned despite himself. “You’ve just bought us the first quiet shift in years.”\\n\\n“Quiet’s the point.”\\n\\nShe left him to it and moved down the hall to the posting board. The old notices hung gray with thumbprints and oil. She pulled them down one by one: *Self-certify if standing,* *Return to work when pulse steadies,* *No pay for rest.* Those went in the bin.\\n\\nIn their place she tacked a single sheet, new ink still glossy. The droplet seal pressed deep, blue. The heading read: **CLINIC REST POLICY — CONCLAVE MANDATE, EFFECTIVE IMMEDIATELY.**\\n\\nShe read it once aloud for herself, because rules always sounded better spoken:\\n\\n> *After any synchronization, local hold, or Vein strain exceeding one Bell, all registered workers will rest for one Bell minimum, paid at standing rate. Witness signatures valid as proof of completion. Non-compliance invalidates later claims. Law prefers you alive.*\\n\\nA few workers nearby paused to listen. One—Jhace, half-bandaged and trying to pretend he wasn’t limping—stopped mid-step.\\n\\n“Since when does law prefer me alive?” he asked.\\n\\n“Since I wrote it that way.”\\n\\nTiffani appeared behind him, still pale, still stubborn. “You think anyone’s going to follow that?”\\n\\nSalee met her gaze. “If it’s posted, it happens. That’s how this city works.”\\n\\nJhace touched the new sheet with one gloved finger. “You Tempermarked it yourself?”\\n\\n“Yes.”\\n\\n“Brighlee sign it?”\\n\\n“She will.”\\n\\nHe nodded, a slow, tired thing. “Then I’ll sleep.”\\n\\nTiffani stared at him like he’d spoken foreign. “You don’t sleep.”\\n\\n“I do now. Policy.”\\n\\nSalee smiled, the kind reserved for things that shouldn’t feel good but do. “That’s the point.”\\n\\nBehind her, the medic’s voice rose: “Two down, one waking, one arguing!”\\n\\n“Sedate the arguer,” Salee said without turning.\\n\\nJhace smirked. “You’d do well in the Underface.”\\n\\n“I already am,” she said. “I just keep my hands clean.”\\n\\nThe rain intensified, striking the windows in thick patterns. The building’s pulse—a subtle vibration from the heat feeds—had settled to a steady level. No flickers, no tremors. For the first time in a long while, Te’Oga felt like it was resting too.\\n\\nSalee gathered the discarded notices, stacked them neat, and burned them in the small brazier by the wall. The smoke rose slow, smelled of ink and old inked lies.\\n\\nFrom her desk she could see the new placard through the hallway glass. Workers were already stopping to read it, some tracing the droplet mark with careful fingers as if it might smudge.\\n\\nBy Bell Six, the clinic was half-empty, not because they’d gone back to work, but because they’d finally gone home to sleep.\\n\\n\\nContinuity:\\nArtifact — **CLINIC REST POLICY — Bell 5** Mandatory paid rest period of one Bell for all registered Setters and Glasses following synchronization, local hold, or Vein strain events. Proof via witness signature. Enforcement delegated to Conclave clinics.\\nImage — The new placard drying on the clinic wall, droplet mark deep blue, steam from the brazier curling around it while rain draws thin silver lines down the window beyond—Te’Oga paused, breathing quiet.\\n\\n---\\n\\n# Chapter 15: The Line Holds\\n\\nBell One came with no alarms. No flicker on the Tempermarks, no pressure twitch beneath the stone. Just morning.\\n\\nJhace stood at the edge of Blackgate again, the place that had nearly drowned him. The air smelled of steam and wet iron, same as ever. But the line on the plate stayed dull and even, cool to the touch. His palm, scarred from that day, fit over the setter’s mark perfectly.\\n\\nTiffani joined him carrying two mugs of the clinic’s bitter tea. “Thought you’d still be asleep.”\\n\\n“Didn’t like the quiet,” he said.\\n\\n“You wrote half the rules that made it.”\\n\\nHe sipped, grimaced. “Rules never taste good.”\\n\\n“Neither does living past your shift.” She leaned on the railing. “Conclave says we’re clear for a round. No global flags, no feeds calling for trace.”\\n\\n“First time in what, twenty rounds?”\\n\\n“Longer.”\\n\\nThey watched the carts roll through the gate. The droplet placard above them caught the sunlight, blue and steady. Drivers touched their caps when they passed, not out of respect, just habit. That was good enough.\\n\\nBrighlee arrived late, for once without her ledger. “Nothing to post today,” she said. “Feels wrong.”\\n\\n“Paper will find you soon enough,” Tiffani said.\\n\\nBrighlee smiled. “Until then, I’m going to the market. Corlexi’s burning another round of old forms. Says he’s doing it for luck.”\\n\\nJhace nodded. “Tell him to keep one. For reference.”\\n\\nShe left with a wave, coat flapping in the wind that always found a way through the stones.\\n\\nThe bridge quieted again. Only the faint sound of water underfoot.\\n\\n“You ever think it’s done?” Tiffani asked.\\n\\n“What.”\\n\\n“The city. The breaking. The patching.”\\n\\nHe watched the seam at their feet. “No. It’s like breathing. You hold too long, you suffocate. You let go too fast, you drown.”\\n\\n“Good thing we’re paid by the breath.”\\n\\nHe laughed once, a real sound this time.\\n\\nDown the span, Mel Quarr inspected a railing, her posture calm. She saw them and raised a hand—no salute, just acknowledgment. The kind of gesture shared by people who survived the same hours.\\n\\n“Mel looks almost peaceful,” Tiffani said.\\n\\n“She earned it.”\\n\\n“So did you.”\\n\\nHe shook his head. “I just didn’t die doing it.”\\n\\nShe didn’t argue. Instead she set her mug on the post and stepped closer. The space between them stayed work-small—close enough to hand off a tool, not quite touch.\\n\\n“Stay,” she said.\\n\\nHe didn’t move. The body obeyed what the mouth said. Her hand found his sleeve, light. His pulse slowed to match the city’s steady level.\\n\\nThey stood like that until the next cart passed, its wheels clicking over the faint ridge of the old trace.\\n\\n“You ever going to finish that cup?” she asked.\\n\\n“Maybe next Bell.”\\n\\n“Policy says rest after success.”\\n\\n“Then I’m already in compliance.”\\n\\nShe smiled without showing teeth, turned toward the Underface stairs. He followed, the morning light cutting low through the mist.\\n\\nHalfway down, he stopped, looked back once. The wet seam along the gate’s mouth gleamed a single shade darker than the plate, steam rising faintly from it—like a living thing still deciding whether to sleep or keep working.\\n\\n\\nContinuity:\\nArtifact — **POST-CLEAR REPORT — Bell 1** All primary Veins stable under dynamic monitoring. No crossline conflict detected. Conclave confirms full operational balance. Work orders zero pending.\\nImage — A steady bead of condensation sliding down the setter’s mark on Blackgate’s plate, sunlight striking it mid-fall until it vanishes into the stone—no drama, no ceremony, just a city held in its own quiet balance.\"}"
        },

    "57867031-671a-4027-9b85-9a1ffc2bd461": {
      "children": [

      ],
      "id": "57867031-671a-4027-9b85-9a1ffc2bd461",

          "content_type": "text",
          "parts": [
            "# Chapter 2: Countermark Line\n\nBell 4 slid through the gate arch like a wire drawn through a die. Jhace had his shoulder to the left leaf, hand on the home ring, eyes on the pier stone ticks that had drifted out of truth. Tiffani brought the float jar up in both hands, glass clinking soft against her thumb ring. The river sat high after last night’s push; you could feel the lift in the chain through the stone underfoot.\n\n“Half arc,” she said.\n\nBrighlee relayed to the winch. Chain cleared slack. The leaf came off its rest and moved into water slow and even. Air pressed cool against their necks where the channel breathed through the gap. Jhace set a chalk string at the sill and paid it out across the pier face to catch the sweep. He watched the leaf’s heel skim the new bow they’d laid that morning. Not a whisper. Good.\n\nTiffani set the float jar on the sill and sighted past it along the two old pins hammered into the far pier seasons ago. Both pins were a touch low against the float’s line; winter had let the channel drop them a hair, or else somebody’s hammer work had been lazy the last time. Either way, their marks looked wrong now.\n\n“Hold,” Tiffani called. The leaf stopped at the throat.\n\nShe took the marker wedge, stood on the sill lip, and tapped a small scribe mark on the pier stone where the float said true. Jhace chalked across it so it would show through spray. First operation placed: a new Countermark line, not guesswork.\n\n“Bring her up a palm,” she said.\n\nThe winch creaked. Water hauled itself through the gap and hissed along the leaf’s upstream face. The float trembled, then steadied. Tiffani set a second mark to bracket the arc. If the hinge lied, the space between marks would change under load. It didn’t.\n\n“Field calm,” she said, keeping the glass to her eye. “We’re pinning this.”\n\nJhace checked his tool roll. Two tempered pins with mushroom heads, a bit that would cut stone without blowing the edge, a bellows to keep the dust honest, the small jar of resin to wet the hole. He grabbed the drill brace and braced his knee against the pier.\n\nChloen came in under the arch with the Conclave’s seal box tucked under her arm. “Warden says pin only with set witness. She doesn’t want some apprentice drilling her pier just because they can spell their names.”\n\n“She’ll get a witness,” Tiffani said, not taking her eye off the float. “Mel Quarr signed the first splice. She’s still on shore.”\n\nChloen disappeared again with her too-fast feet. Brighlee, in the habit of counting bells against paperwork, opened the blue ledger and set it on the Posting board’s cap so spray wouldn’t soak the page edges. “Entry started. Countermark proposed. Blackgate left leaf. Bell 4 half.”\n\nJhace sighted the bit’s nose against the chalk tick and held his breath while he began to turn. Stone complained with a dry bite, then settled into a rhythm. He kept the brace vertical and steady, letting the bit nibble without forcing. Too much pressure and the hole would cone. He pumped the bellows with his off hand and sneezed dust out of his nose without taking the bit off line.\n\n“Depth one thumb,” Tiffani said, watching his wrist where it cast a small shadow across the float. She could read depth in that shadow the way some people read faces. “No more or you’ll split the old layer.”\n\nHe nodded once and counted the turns. The brace warmed under his palm. He pulled the bit and blew the hole. Dust fluffed out and settled on the river wet like flour.\n\n“Resin,” he said.\n\nTiffani snapped the resin jar seal with the edge of her thumbnail and wicked a line into the hole with a slender brush. The resin drank and went dark. She tapped the excess away so no skin would grow proud and catch current later.\n\n“Pin,” she said.\n\nJhace set the first pin, head cupped in his left hand so it wouldn’t skate. He brought the hammer face square and tapped it in short beats that lived in the root of his wrist. The pin slid clean and locked with a sound like a dry nail finding old wood. He rocked the head to test set. It didn’t turn. He stamped the head with the small die he carried for this only: BG-L-4-2. Blackgate left, bell four, second season.\n\n“Second hole,” Tiffani said.\n\nHe drilled the match head width above the first bracket, two hands to the brace, breath slow. The second pin set easier. Metal kissed stone with air trapped in a small sigh. He stamped its head to match. The float line looked like a thread drawn taut over glass.\n\nMel Quarr’s boots clattered down the steps. The setter shook river spray off her cuffs and looked once at the float, once at the pins, once at Jhace. “Witness present. No HALT.”\n\n“Write it,” Brighlee said, pen already moving.\n\nThey weren’t done. A pin was a point. A Countermark was a direction carried through time. Tiffani snapped a chalk line between the two pin heads and set it tight against the pier. She snapped the line to lay chalk smooth into the grain, then took out the small scoring chisel to cut that powder into a channel a hair deep. She did not push for beauty. She pushed for a line that water wouldn’t wash clean in the first rain.\n\n“Shade one, just to warm the skin,” she said.\n\nJhace lit the smallest coal and moved it in a lazy circle near the stone, enough heat to dry the chalk into the first two layers of the pier. The air smelled like warm river and old mortar. He lifted the heat away before the line could bake brittle.\n\nThey rolled the leaf across the new line and watched the heel pass. It held true within the chalk. The pins sat where the float said to sit. Tiffani set her hand on the plate and felt nothing new.\n\n“Mark the heel,” she said.\n\nHe knelt by the hinge and etched a small arrow into the iron, not for decoration. Arrows let the next body know what this body had believed. He ticked the date again under the arrow with the same shallow V he’d used in the morning.\n\nTraffic squeezed against the north rope. A courier with sand on his boots looked like he wanted to say something, then didn’t, because the droplet icon still hung from its clip and men who valued their wheels respected the signs. Tiffani rolled her shoulders and stood, frowning at the right leaf now.\n\n“Something?” Jhace asked.\n\n“Right leaf throws a fake lean when the south wind comes through this throat. It hums hot. Not today,” she said, sniffing the air like she could taste heat in it. “We’ll put that on the list for lean season.”\n\n“Clinic,” Brighlee said to Jhace, practical as paper. “You said ring into Bell 4. Is Bell 4.”\n\n“It’s small,” he said.\n\n“Policy,” Tiffani said, and looked at his ear with her head tilted. “Go after we post.”\n\nThe chain team on the winch waited with hands resting loose on the bar. Water ran as if it had never done anything else. The float jar’s lines wavered because of air, not because of work. Tiffani handed the jar to Chloen to box. Chloen’s quick hands slid glass into felt and shut it with that special I-know-what-this-is worth care she sometimes remembered.\n\n“Write the board,” Tiffani told Brighlee.\n\nBrighlee did, square hand like a plate edge. “Countermark set Blackgate left pier at Bell 4 half. Two tempered pins stamped. Line cut. Witness: Setter Quarr. No HALT. Arc held.”\n\nTiffani stepped back and looked at the Posting board, then at the pier, then at the water. The same three checks she did every time. When the checks matched, she exhaled, and his own back let go. The tremor that wanted to practice in his little finger didn’t make a case. They fed their kits back into their own pockets where the weights lived right.\n\n“Float back to Conclave,” Mel Quarr said, already half up the steps. “If you drop it, I sign your pay over to the river.”\n\n“Understood,” Chloen piped.\n\nJhace wiped his hands on the inside of his belt, where the black wouldn’t show, and leaned close enough to the pins that he could see the ring of resin. No voids. No chatter marks. Stone and metal married clean.\n\n“Open for Bell,” Tiffani called.\n\nThey took the leaf to rest and ran the rope in. Carts started through. Someone spat a seed hull into the water and then stiffened when Mel Quarr looked back down from the stairs with a setter’s stare. Hulls did damage in underways when they piled in corners. The boy who had spat went red and kicked the hull into the river properly. Paper and stares moved bodies.\n\nChloen handed up the seal box. Tiffani pressed the blue circle onto the work notice and held it there until the pigment took an even skin. She made sure the edge didn’t pool and the center didn’t blur. Jhace felt the relief come into the air when the circle released his part of the morning like heat leaving a plate set back into shade.\n\nThey walked the stones once more out of habit, checking nothing because that was the job. The pins shone a little in the thin light that squeezed through the arch. The chalk line lay straight as a drawn wire.\n\nThey put their names where the law wanted them.\n\nPublic artifact:\n\nBLACKGATE COUNTERMARK NOTICE, posted Bell 4 half  \nTwo tempered pins set on left pier to fix arc reference for Blackgate left leaf. Line scored and heat-set.  \nGlass float aligned to far pier pins; arc holds at half and throat.  \nNo HALT. Witness: Setter Mel Quarr (Conclave).  \nSignatories: Tiffani Veld (Outer Crown), Jhace Brast (Underface).  \nEffective at posting. Couriers’ Bell docket entry noted.\n\nConcrete image: twin pin heads flush in the pier stone with a chalk line running taut between them, river light wobbling on the glass jar where the float rests empty beside the sill."
          ]
        },

    "589b42d0-89fe-4cc3-b2d6-f8fe5484a94d": {
      "children": [
        "06f458b1-ee00-4095-b819-448c72f39f43"
      ],
      "id": "589b42d0-89fe-4cc3-b2d6-f8fe5484a94d",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": null,
          "role": "assistant"
        },
        "channel": null,
        "content": {
          "content_type": "text",
          "parts": [
            "# Chapter 13: Mid-Span Seal  \n\nBell 15 carried thick through the arch, the kind that made air hum before metal joined in. Water at the mid-span licked the gate’s seam in a pattern you didn’t want to hear twice—soft at first, then sharp, like breath through a split tooth. Jhace dropped to the center platform and braced a hand on the stone lip. He could feel the water’s pulse through the plate.  \n\n“Not surface,” he said.  \n\nTiffani nodded once. “Mid-seal creep. Two flats, Shade 2. We close it before it writes itself bigger.”  \n\nBrighlee read from the fresh slip so everyone had the rule before they saw the work. “Mid-span seal, inner face, no HALT. Witness to be fetched.” She clipped the card under the droplet and stepped back. The line of carts paused without complaint; it wasn’t bravery, just routine.  \n\nJhace slid the Lume under the lip. Light spilled back in a slant that showed silver, not black. The seal’s lower edge had lifted half a thumb off the plate; water wrote itself under the corner and spit tiny bubbles. “One finger lift,” he said.  \n\n“Chain,” Tiffani called.  \n\nThe crew took weight till the seam cleared water line. The exposed metal gleamed like a small wound.  \n\n“Heat?” she asked.  \n\nHe pressed the back of his glove to the plate. Warm from friction, not rot. “Safe,” he said.  \n\n“Cut the lifted edge.”  \n\nHe took the short knife from his belt, slid it under the loose flat, and drew one clean stroke to free the failed corner. The old Veil skin peeled back, grain even, failure along glue not Shade—honest decay. He set the scrap aside for Brighlee’s pouch.  \n\n“Sand the seat,” Tiffani said.  \n\nHe ran the fine grit stone along the seat until it wore dull and even. He wiped dust into the river with one motion. “Bare.”  \n\nShe opened the Veil case, selected two Shade 2 flats, and warmed them over the brazier spoon until they softened. Steam rose and caught the smell of old resin. She knelt, one knee on the plate, and set the first flat against the seat.  \n\n“Hold pressure,” she said.  \n\nJhace pressed his palm over the flat, slow and steady. The warmth carried through his glove. The flat conformed, surface tension doing the quiet work of law.  \n\n“Tap run,” she said.  \n\nHe tapped the edge with the hammer heel, seven count, pause, seven. Resin darkened and cooled. The seal took. She placed the second flat overlapping the first by a finger, warmed the joint with breath, and nodded.  \n\n“Slake.”  \n\nHe thumbed the bladder cap, wet the joint, pressure from shoulder only, two fingers’ worth. Steam lifted in a fine sheet, gone before it found light. The smell changed—heat and metal and faint oil—good smell, not warning.  \n\n“Test,” she said.  \n\nBrighlee signaled. Chain bled slow. Water pressed up under the new flats and met resistance. The first bead formed, held, didn’t bubble. Jhace touched the edge; it was cool, not false cool, the real kind that meant the bond was done working.  \n\n“Veil line holds,” he said.  \n\n“Edge guard,” Tiffani said.  \n\nHe brushed the outer lip with copper wash, thin as ink, to keep air from chewing it. The copper took immediately, changing color from bright to coin dull.  \n\nMel Quarr’s boots struck the stair like punctuation. “Seal work?”  \n\n“Mid-span, inner face,” Tiffani said. “Old Veil lifted. Seat sanded. Two Shade 2 flats Slaked and coppered. No HALT.”  \n\nMel crouched and set her finger to the edge of the fresh seal. “Overlap even. Bead tight.” She pressed harder, testing for lift. None. “Witness. Note joint length.”  \n\n“Eight hands from post,” Jhace said.  \n\nBrighlee wrote it square. “Mid-span seal inner face, eight-hand run, two Shade 2 flats Slaked, copper wash. Witness Quarr. Bell 15.”  \n\nTiffani checked the float. “Line steady. Plumb?”  \n\nJhace tapped the bob. Centered on tick. “On.”  \n\n“Full lift to test compression,” she said.  \n\nThey took the chain up. The leaf climbed until the water line dropped off the plate’s edge. The new seal shone dull under Lume. He watched the joint’s surface; no lift, no sweat.  \n\n“Drop half,” she said.  \n\nThe leaf lowered. Water returned, pressed, and found no purchase. The bubbles stopped. He pressed his palm flat on the metal until he could feel nothing but the pulse of the river beyond. The world steadied.  \n\n“Mark it,” she said.  \n\nHe cut a small double line at the upstream corner and pressed his die: BG-MS-15. He rubbed the burr flat.  \n\n“Cost?” she asked.  \n\n“Heat bite on palm. Forearm steady. No ring,” he said.  \n\nShe held her thumb over the spot till the heat eased. “Clinic if it blisters.”  \n\nHe nodded.  \n\nChloen had the seal box open already. Tiffani pressed the blue circle onto the slip, counted to three, checked edges, pressed once more to catch a dry corner. She clipped it under the droplet. The new circle dried while the river kept breathing.  \n\nMel Quarr stood without dusting her knees. “Gate’s sound. You’ll hear the difference in the next lift.”  \n\nTiffani nodded. “We’ll log the pitch.”  \n\nMel signed the docket and went back up.  \n\n“Open to half for sound,” Tiffani said.  \n\nThe winch bar turned. Chain came live. The gate lifted. Water found the new seam and stayed silent. He could feel the difference through the soles of his boots: the gate’s hum had dropped half a note.  \n\nBrighlee wrote that down too; she kept every sound.  \n\nThe first cart in line had started its horses before the rope even lifted; they knew the rhythm. The wheel passed the mid-span joint with no jolt. The driver glanced down out of habit, then away—no squeal meant no story to tell later.  \n\nJhace cleaned the tools, stacking them by touch and weight. Tiffani sealed the Veil case. Chloen wiped the copper wash from her glove before it could harden. Brighlee folded the ledger closed, thumb between pages to keep it breathing until dry.  \n\n“Season mark?” she asked.  \n\nTiffani looked at the seal. “Dry season two, blackstone arc.”  \n\nBrighlee wrote it at the bottom of the page.  \n\nThey stood a count while river light moved over the new metal. The seam sat even; the copper wash threw back dull reflection; the line held.  \n\nWork complete, not fixed—fixed meant it could fail again. This one was joined.  \n\n---\n\n**Public artifact:**  \n**BLACKGATE MID-SPAN SEAL NOTICE — Bell 15**  \nInner-face seal renewed mid-span, eight-hand run. Old Veil removed, seat sanded, two Shade 2 flats Slaked, copper wash applied at edge.  \nArc and pressure tested to half and full lift; float and plumb confirm.  \nNo HALT. Witness: Setter Mel Quarr (Conclave).  \nSignatories: Tiffani Veld (Outer Crown), Jhace Brast (Underface).  \nFollow-up: record gate pitch next rise cycle.  \nDocket entry made at Couriers’ Bell.  \n\n**Concrete image:** a dull copper strip lying flat along the gate’s center seam, water pressing against it without a sound, thin bubbles caught and held as light ripples across the face."
          ]
        },
        "create_time": 1761018829.047045,
        "end_turn": true,

    "59377e49-019b-4a3f-9c35-4caead1621b1": {
      "children": [
        "bebe39f3-7e37-4520-99f7-d8beb2c7c191"
      ],
      "id": "59377e49-019b-4a3f-9c35-4caead1621b1",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": null,
          "role": "assistant"
        },
        "channel": "final",
        "content": {
          "content_type": "text",
          "parts": [
            "# Chapter 6: Top Stop Copper Cap\n\nBell 8 hit hard off the Couriers’ stone. Right leaf kissed rest with a knock that traveled the post. Jhace laid three fingers on the top stop and felt the bite mark the nose had carved over a season of wind. Tiffani raised her palm. “Hold one finger off rest.”\n\nBrighlee relayed. Chain caught and held. The leaf floated a hair from the stop, no sag. Tiffani pulled the soot pot from her case, smoked the stop face until it wore a thin black skin, and nodded.\n\n“Touch and back,” she said.\n\nJhace signaled the winch. The leaf closed to a kiss and came back out. Soot transferred in a crescent high on the stop and blank low. The nose hit too tight, too high. That bought a knock and a slow grind that would turn into misalignment before the rains.\n\n“Planish the crown,” she said.\n\nHe took the planishing hammer and stroked the stop face with short clean strikes, not heavy. Iron raised the skin, then laid down even. He checked with a straightedge cut from glass, a true strip with ground edges. Light showed at the lower quarter. He dressed that, too, with the flat file until the glass lay without daylight.\n\n“Lampblack again,” Tiffani said.\n\nHe smoked the face, kissed the leaf, backed it. Transfer now came in two smaller crescents, high and low. He wanted a broad even print when it closed, so weight would dissipate across the face and stop the knock.\n\n“Copper cap,” she said. “We spread load.”\n\nChloen brought the short copper bar from the strap, annealed last winter and skinned in oil. Jhace measured the stop face with a strip of stiff card and transferred the shape to metal. He cut the cap with the cold shear, edges square, corners rounded so they wouldn’t raise burrs against sleeves or cuffs. He warmed the piece over the brazier spoon until the oxide went from orange to a mild red, then drew it out of heat before it slumped. No quench. He set it to the stop and persuaded the curve with the wooden mallet until the fit sat clean. No daylight under the edges when he lifted the glass strip across it.\n\n“Three pins,” Tiffani said. “No drift in stone.”\n\nHe marked the cap for two top pins and one lower. He drilled the stop plate, not the pier, with the short bit that kept its line in iron. He kept oil at the hole mouth and used the bellows to lift swarf between cuts. Holes ran true. He brushed them clean. Tiffani wicked a whisper of resin only at the interface where cap met plate, not in the holes, to close any breath lines that would drink water.\n\n“Pins,” he said.\n\nShe handed him three tempered pins with shallow heads. He pressed the cap into place, held it with his left hand, and tapped the first pin home with the hammer face square. It seated with a tight note and stayed. The second took the same. At the lower hole he felt a chatter through the handle that told of a burr left from drill. He backed the pin, stroked the hole with the reamer three quarters of a turn, blew it, and set the pin. Clean seat. Heads flush.\n\n“Dress the nose,” Tiffani said.\n\nHe filed the gate nose just enough to remove the bright lip that had been making the knock. One pass too many and you invite play; he took two, checked the glass, and stopped. He smoked the new cap, then brought the leaf in to rest.\n\n“Hold,” she said.\n\nThe soot print came even across the face. No crescent gaps. No point hit. She set her ear near the joint and listened to air change where iron met copper. No scratch. “Again.”\n\nHe opened an inch and let it come closed. The leaf met the cap and laid without chatter. The knock died. The throat sound dropped back to a flat rush.\n\nMel Quarr came down the steps with her slip open, blue cord around her wrist. “What did you sell to the stop?”\n\n“Copper cap on top stop face,” Tiffani said. “Three pins into plate. Planish on iron before fit. Nose dress two strokes. No move into pier.”\n\nMel put her fingers on the cap and then on the plate, then on the pin heads. She pressed the nose with the heel of her hand and walked the leaf in by a thumb until it kissed. “Witness. No HALT,” she said. “Post.”\n\nBrighlee wrote square on the board slip while holding the ledger under the cap out of spray. “Top stop faced with copper cap. Pins three. Nose dress. Planish. No HALT. Bell 8. Witness Quarr.” She said it aloud so the queue would catch the law with their ears.\n\n“Seal,” Tiffani said.\n\nChloen had the stamp ready. Tiffani pressed the blue circle, held it to count three so the pigment grabbed, then clipped the notice under the droplet icon. Paper fixed bodies. Teamsters eased their hands on brake boards. A boy in a brown cap counted the pins without needing to get told to keep fingers back.\n\n“Cost?” she asked Jhace without looking away from the cap.\n\n“Wrist heat. Thumb pad buzz. Ear quiet,” he said.\n\nShe set two fingers against his wrist and waited for the buzz to leave. It left. She pulled her hand back and rolled her shoulder.\n\n“Open for test,” she said.\n\nBrighlee signaled the winch. Chain came live. The leaf rolled off rest, then closed to rest. No knock on closing. No scrape at the top bar. He watched the gauge wire they’d left from Bell 5. No flick when it kissed. He set his palm on the cap after the second close. Warm from friction, not hot. Copper spread the load and gave nothing back but the right heat.\n\n“Mark it,” Tiffani said.\n\nHe took the scribe and cut a short bar mark on the stop plate at the cap’s lower right corner. He stamped the cap with the tiny die that said BG-R-C. Blackgate right, cap. He rubbed the burr until the stamp lay smooth.\n\n“Float check rest,” she said.\n\nHe set the float jar on the sill. She sighted to the far pins. At rest the line lay dead. She nodded, once. “Plumb.”\n\nHe touched the plumb to the pier tick. Bob centered and held. That told the story of rest without speech. She exhaled and the tension walked out of the air between his shoulder blades like heat leaving a plate taken off a brazier.\n\n“Chain,” Jhace said, glancing to the right run at link 21. It still wore polish from last bell. “Note stands. Rotate at low water.”\n\n“Ledger keeps it,” Brighlee said, drawing a small box around the earlier note.\n\nThey walked the cap and nose with eyes and fingers the way you check glass for a scratch. The cap edges lay flat. Pins sat flush. The soot print kept to a broad, even band. The stop wore a new face and the leaf respected it.\n\nA barge horn upriver sent a pulse through air. The queue looked. Tiffani did not. She studied the cap and then the water under it and then the board. Three checks. They matched. She set the soot pot back in her case, lid tight, because one small act prevents ten dirty sleeves later.\n\nChloen stretched her arm out with the mesh hook by habit, then pulled it back when she remembered what job they were at. She peered up at the copper. “Shiny,” she said, then looked guilty for saying anything that simple.\n\n“Shiny now,” Jhace answered, not unkind. “Brown by end of week. It’s the work that holds.”\n\nMel Quarr’s boot heel knocked once on the step in a pattern that meant she was ready to leave. “Cap reads true. If it loosens you’ll hear it before I do. Tighten before you hear it twice.”\n\n“We tighten before once,” Tiffani said.\n\nThey cycled the leaf twice more, slow, then at travel speed. Copper took the touch and spread it. Iron nose left a clean kiss on soot, even. No flake. No smear.\n\n“Post,” Tiffani said again, because law rides repetition.\n\nBrighlee clipped a copy of the notice for the Couriers’ runner to carry. She wrote the docket line where runners could read it in less time than it takes to step around a wheel hub. “Top stop copper cap fitted. Pins three. Nose dressed. Arc unchanged.” She signed, handed the pen. Tiffani signed. Jhace signed.\n\nThe rope came in. The first cart crossed. The iron rim passed under the cap’s shadow and over the hinge guard without talking. A second cart followed, wheel grease cold enough to hold shape. The river pressed through the throat and kept its flat run. The stop face stayed quiet.\n\nThey walked the stones from nose to hinge and back. Hands checked what eyes might miss. The world did not care. They made it care.\n\nPublic artifact:\n\nBLACKGATE TOP STOP FACE NOTICE, posted Bell 8  \nRight leaf stop faced with copper cap, three tempered pins set into plate; stop planished before cap fit. Nose dressed two file strokes to remove bright lip. Rest tested; arc and plumb hold.  \nNo HALT. Witness: Setter Mel Quarr (Conclave).  \nSignatories: Tiffani Veld (Outer Crown), Jhace Brast (Underface).  \nDocket entry made at Couriers’ Bell.\n\nConcrete image: a fresh copper cap tight against the iron stop, three flush pin heads catching thin river light, the leaf nose closing to leave one even soot kiss across the face."
          ]
        },
        "create_time": 1761013000.59166,
        "end_turn": true,

    "5eec5f2f-c523-4bba-b8c7-47ed41412b30": {
      "children": [
        "77eed696-f1e3-4a77-b80c-11921003ba87"
      ],
      "id": "5eec5f2f-c523-4bba-b8c7-47ed41412b30",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": null,
          "role": "assistant"
        },
        "channel": null,
        "content": {
          "content_type": "text",
          "parts": [
            "# **Chapter 12: Crossline at Ledger Row**\n\nBell Three came in dry for once, cold air cutting through the Underface like clean glass. It was almost pleasant until Jhace saw the smoke curling from under the boards along Ledger Row. The smell wasn’t fire—it was burnt resin and oil, the kind of heat you only got when Veins fought each other.  \n\nTiffani was already kneeling by the conduit hatch, hair pulled back, eyes narrow from fatigue and focus. “Feed maps say one line,” she said, voice tight. “There are two.”  \n\nJhace dropped to a crouch beside her, pressing his good hand against the iron face. The vibration was wild. Two distinct pulses, crossing in opposite rhythms, one slow and hot, one quick and cold. “Crossline. Someone overlaid without bleed.”  \n\n“That’s not possible without Conclave clearance.”  \n\n“Or a bribe.”  \n\nBrighlee arrived half-running, panting. “Work order five-zero-seven, feed interference, Ledger Row. No HALT posted.” She scanned the crowd gathering at the edge. “We might need one.”  \n\n“Not yet,” Tiffani said. “Half the quarter’s power runs through here. If we stop it, clinics freeze before Bell Six.”  \n\nJhace nodded once. “We keep it live. Slow fix.”  \n\n“Slow’ll kill us if the Veins argue harder.”  \n\n“They’re already arguing.”  \n\nHe pried the hatch open. The inside glowed faintly red-blue, two colors flickering at war. The Veins had touched somewhere beneath, fusing in a way that was both alive and dying.  \n\n“Shade three,” he said.  \n\nTiffani adjusted the Lume, washing the chamber in muted amber light. “There’s a junction below the ledger feed, looks welded by hand.”  \n\nHe leaned in, saw the crude cross of metal binding both veins together. “That’s not a weld. That’s an apology.”  \n\n“Someone knew it was wrong and hoped it’d hold.”  \n\n“Hope isn’t a method.”  \n\nHe reached in with the trace rod, testing. Sparks spat up the length of it.  \n\n“Don’t touch the join,” she said sharply. “You’ll wake it.”  \n\n“Too late.” The hum shifted. Both pulses synchronized for one heartbeat, then slipped again, a deep, grinding whine that made the floor shake.  \n\nBrighlee stumbled back, ledger clutched to her chest. “That sounded like—”  \n\n“Backflow,” Jhace said. “It’s feeding both directions.”  \n\n“Then it’s gonna eat itself.”  \n\n“Not if we teach it manners.”  \n\nHe pulled his gloves tight and slid the trace rod deeper. Heat bit through the leather. “Give me wet, low pressure.”  \n\nTiffani passed him the bladder. “Half-beat Slake.”  \n\n“Half-beat,” he echoed.  \n\nHe eased a thin line along the hot vein first. Steam poured out, hissed, then bent. The color dimmed. “It’s listening.”  \n\n“Cold side next,” she said.  \n\nHe shifted the rod, drew a second line that ran parallel but in reverse direction. The two wet trails hissed where they met, forming a pale seam.  \n\n“It’s working,” Brighlee said softly.  \n\n“Not yet.” Jhace pressed his burned palm flat against the plate. The pain kept him sharp. “Give me Shade one. I want the edges honest.”  \n\nTiffani dropped the light, fine and close. The flicker slowed. The noise eased into a low, even hum.  \n\n“That’s the sweet spot,” she whispered.  \n\nHe didn’t move. “It’s temporary. The weld underneath is still there.”  \n\nShe nodded. “Then we peel it.”  \n\nThey worked in silence, two sets of hands moving like one, prying at the crooked cross until the metal gave with a faint groan. Beneath it, the two Veins lay clean but raw. Jhace laid a single wet bead between them, sealing the scar.  \n\nMel Quarr appeared in the doorway, coat dripping from street wash. “What did you two touch this time?”  \n\n“Crossline under Ledger,” Tiffani said. “Unauthorized weld.”  \n\nMel crouched, studying the seal. “You joined them?”  \n\n“Separated them,” Jhace said. “Then taught them to agree.”  \n\nMel’s mouth tightened. “That’s not procedure.”  \n\n“It’s what worked.”  \n\nShe pressed her ear to the conduit wall, listening. “Sounds stable.” She straightened, slower than usual. “You realize if that weld had held another Bell, it would’ve burned through to the counting halls?”  \n\n“Then we’re early,” Jhace said.  \n\nMel smiled without humor. “Luck counts as skill in my book. Witnessed.”  \n\nBrighlee wrote fast, her handwriting going sloppy from wet fingers. “Ledger Row, crossline repair, dual trace confirmed.”  \n\n“Add note,” Mel said. “Unauthorized weld under review. Materials confiscated.”  \n\n“Already did.”  \n\nThe rain outside picked up, washing soot down the gutters.  \n\nTiffani leaned back, rubbing her face. “That makes four unregistered modifications in a week.”  \n\nJhace wiped his tools clean. “Pattern’s too neat to be chance.”  \n\n“You think it’s one guild?”  \n\n“I think it’s everyone who thinks they can hide a shortcut.”  \n\nMel stepped toward the door. “Write it down,” she said. “Patterns matter.”  \n\nBrighlee nodded. “Filed.”  \n\nThe crowd began to move again, carts edging forward as the tension lifted. A vendor shouted something about late deliveries. The world resumed its noise.  \n\nJhace packed his tools. “You realize we’re just bailing a sinking ship with prettier buckets.”  \n\nTiffani closed her case. “Then we make the buckets shine.”  \n\nHe looked at her, the corner of his mouth twitching. “You’d polish a corpse.”  \n\n“Only if it earned it.”  \n\nMel lingered in the doorway, watching them. “You two keep that attitude and the Conclave will write you into the walls.”  \n\n“Then at least they’ll stay up,” Jhace said.  \n\nMel left first. The others followed.  \n\nWhen the street cleared, the repaired conduit steamed softly in the cold air. Two lines, once at war, now hummed in rhythm under the stone.  \n\n---\n\n**Public artifact:**  \n**LEDGER ROW CROSSLlNE — Bell 3–5**  \nUnauthorized dual-feed junction discovered beneath accounting district. Illegal weld excised, dual trace and reseal executed under live flow. Feed stabilized.  \nWitness: Mel Quarr (Conclave).  \nSignatories: Jhace brast (Underface), Tiffani veld (Outer Crown).  \nCosts: fatigue, minor reinjury (hand), tool heat wear.  \nPolicy addendum: cross-feed junctions require dual oversight and random audit within five Bells of modification notice.  \n\n**Concrete image:**  \nSteam rising from twin Veins under the street, the wet seam between them glowing faint blue in the dark, humming like breath caught between agreement and surrender."
          ]
        },
        "create_time": 1761021885.859016,
        "end_turn": true,
        "id": "5eec5f2f-c523-4bba-b8c7-47ed41412b30",
